summary: This week in AI, significant developments include OpenAI's new family of models, Apple's lawsuit against OpenAI for alleged trade secret theft, and Meta's removal of a controversial AI feature from Instagram. Open source AI continues to grow, with Hugging Face's CEO emphasizing its importance. Regulatory actions, such as the EU's warning to Meta about autoplay and infinite scroll, also made headlines.
insight: The intersection of AI and ethics is becoming increasingly prominent, with lawsuits over copyright infringement and concerns about AI-generated content, highlighting the need for clearer guidelines and regulations in the AI industry.
highlights: OpenAI launches new family of models with GPT-5.6, promising improvements in cybersecurity and other areas.
Apple sues OpenAI for allegedly stealing hardware secrets, escalating tensions between tech giants.
Meta removes AI feature from Instagram after backlash, demonstrating the challenges of integrating AI into social media platforms.

articles.title: OpenAI bets on families as ChatGPT goes deeper into households
articles.description: ChatGPT is hiring a dedicated product manager to build experiences for families, caregivers, and older adults, according to a job posting.

articles.title: Open source AI matters more than ever, according to Hugging Face’s Clem Delangue

articles.title: OpenAI says GPT 5.6 is the ‘preferred model’ for Microsoft Copilot 365 amid breakup chatter
articles.description: OpenAI's new family of models will continue to power Microsoft's suite of workplace and productivity apps.

articles.title: OpenAI launches its new family of models with GPT-5.6
articles.description: OpenAI's latest family of models promises improvements across a range of areas, including cybersecurity.

articles.title: Humanoid robots controlled by surgeons did world-first operation on live pigs
articles.description: Preclinical trial is testing the feasibility of humanoid robots in surgery.

articles.title: Disable autoplay and infinite scroll or risk massive fines, EU tells Meta
articles.description: Digital Services Act may force Meta to make big changes on its platforms.

articles.title: Instagram’s Adam Mosseri: If you don’t like AI, ‘then you shouldn’t have it in your feed’

articles.title: OpenAI is shutting down Atlas, but its AI browser ambitions are still growing
articles.description: OpenAI is sunsetting its AI-powered browser after less than a year. But it's moving some agentic browsing features to its desktop app and a Chrome extension.

articles.title: Google will now tell you if an ad was made with AI
articles.description: You can see if ads on Google Search, Google Discover, and YouTube were made or edited using AI from a new section in Google's "My Ad Center," as reported earlier by TechCrunch. The

articles.title: Meta’s new AI chips will begin production in September
articles.description: The company is taking a modular approach to designing these chips, anticipating that their needs will change as AI evolves rapidly by the time the chips are in production.
